systems.name: Legality
systems.definition: What the law permits or forbids. Set by statutes, regulations, and courts; varies by jurisdiction and is enforced by the state. The floor, not the ceiling.

systems.name: Ethics
systems.definition: Agreed, often professional, norms of right conduct — what a thoughtful community expects of you (e.g. a clinician's duty of confidentiality). Broader than law and frequently stricter.

systems.name: Morality
systems.definition: Your own and society's sense of right and wrong — conscience, fairness, respect for others. The deepest layer; it asks not 'can I?' but 'should I?'.

coreMessage: Legal does not mean ethical. A recording can be perfectly lawful and still be a betrayal of someone's trust. Before you record, ask whether the people speaking would feel respected — not just whether a statute allows it.

lenses.name: Consequentialism
lenses.question: Do the outcomes of recording do more good than harm, for everyone affected?
lenses.appliedToRecording: Weigh the benefit (a faithful record, accessibility, accountability) against harms (chilled candor, leaked sensitive content, broken trust). If the likely harm outweighs the benefit, reconsider.

lenses.name: Deontology (duties & rights)
lenses.question: Are you respecting people's rights and your duties, regardless of outcome?
lenses.appliedToRecording: People have a right to know they are being recorded and to decline. Recording someone secretly treats them as a means, not an end — even if the result is useful.

lenses.name: Virtue ethics
lenses.question: Would an honest, trustworthy person record this, this way?
lenses.appliedToRecording: Ask what recording says about your character. Openness and candor build trust; covert capture, even when legal, erodes it.

lenses.name: Contextual integrity (Nissenbaum)
lenses.question: Does this recording fit the information norms of the context it happens in?
lenses.appliedToRecording: Privacy is appropriate information flow. Words shared in confidence in therapy, a support group, or among friends carry an expectation that they stay there. Capturing or repurposing them violates the context even if no law is broken.

contextualIntegrity: Helen Nissenbaum's framework treats privacy not as secrecy but as the appropriate flow of information within a context. Every setting — a doctor's office, a team standup, a kitchen-table chat — has entrenched norms about who hears what, and on what terms (in confidence, off the record, just between us). A recording can be legal yet still breach those norms by widening the audience, making the ephemeral permanent, or shifting words from 'in confidence' to 'shareable'. Use it as a practical test: would this recording, and what you plan to do with it, surprise or dismay the people who spoke?

scenarios.title: Secretly recording a therapy or medical conversation
scenarios.whyLegal: In a one-party-consent state, a patient recording their own session may be lawful.
scenarios.whyEthicalProblem: Health and recovery settings carry the strongest expectation of confidentiality. Covert recording betrays a therapeutic relationship and can deter honest disclosure.

scenarios.title: Recording a support group (e.g. AA) confession
scenarios.whyLegal: If you are a participant in a one-party state, recording may be legal.
scenarios.whyEthicalProblem: Anonymity and confidentiality are the entire point of the setting. Recording — and the mere risk of it — destroys the safety the group depends on.

scenarios.title: Recording a friend's vulnerable confession, then sharing it
scenarios.whyLegal: Capturing your own conversation can be lawful; the ethical breach is the use, not just the capture.
scenarios.whyEthicalProblem: The words were shared in confidence between friends. Widening the audience is a contextual-integrity violation and a betrayal of trust, regardless of legality.

scenarios.title: Covertly recording employees
scenarios.whyLegal: Some one-party states permit it; many all-party states do not, and labor law may add constraints.
scenarios.whyEthicalProblem: A power imbalance plus secrecy undermines trust and autonomy. Even where legal, covert workplace recording is corrosive; disclosure and policy are the ethical path.

scenarios.title: Recording from a one-party state to dodge another party's all-party expectation
scenarios.whyLegal: You may sit in a one-party state — but the other party's state law (and Kearney-style reasoning) can still apply.
scenarios.whyEthicalProblem: Deliberately exploiting a jurisdictional gap to avoid someone's reasonable expectation of consent is ethically evasive even if it survives a legal challenge.

scenarios.title: Reusing transcripts for purposes the speaker never imagined
scenarios.whyLegal: Once lawfully recorded, further use is rarely separately regulated (outside data-protection regimes).
scenarios.whyEthicalProblem: Feeding a candid conversation into AI training, performance reviews, or analytics is a 'secondary use' the speaker never agreed to — a contextual-integrity violation distinct from the recording itself.

guidance: Default to disclosure: tell people you are recording, and why, at the start.
Treat sensitive contexts (health, recovery, confession, intimacy, legal jeopardy) as do-not-record-covertly, regardless of the law.
Separate capture from use: get agreement for both recording AND what you will do with the transcript.
If someone seems hesitant, offer an unrecorded alternative — consent under pressure is not real consent.
Ask the contextual-integrity question: would this recording, and your plans for it, surprise the people who spoke?
